dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for fainting or unconsciousness at the --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's wrong today?
The call is for fainting or near-unconsciousness. Let's start by making sure you're comfortable if that's alright with you?
I feel nauseous, and I've been throwing up for the last couple of days-it's not been anything serious, just up and down.
Can you open your eyes and tell me what the date is today? Can you tell me your name and what's going on here?
It's Thursday, I'm Grace, and I'm just not feeling well-though I'm awake and clear in my head.
Great, you're alert and able to interact. You seem aware of person, place, time, and situation. Would you say you are oriented to your surroundings as well?
Yes, I'm aware of where I am, who I am, and what's going on.
She is fully alert and oriented with a GCS score of 15.
Have you noticed any blood in your vomit?
No, just fluid and nausea, mostly when I'm off the oxygen.
That sounds challenging. Does your shortness of breath depend on the oxygen more?
Definitely-my breathing's really hard without it.
Have you previously experienced any abdominal pain or discomfort?
No, I haven't actually-just some discomfort in my chest and lower areas when I'm breathing hard.
Have you experienced any dizziness or headaches alongside the nausea?
No, just general nausea and trouble breathing when off oxygen.
Can you tell me about your medical conditions?
I've had a stroke and am weak on the left. I also have chronic kidney disease-on dialysis three days a week.
Recognizing hemiplegia from stroke and end-stage renal disease. Know anyone nearby who might assist during transfer?
We'll use a two-person pivot for the transfer considering your stroke-related weakness.
When was the last time you had food or fluids in your stomach?
Yesterday, maybe earlier today. I've been so queasy, it makes me stop.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: When the symptoms started, was there anything that might have led to that?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Just fatigue and coming off my dialysis days-I try to eat right, but it still came on slowly.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is the nausea today?
Not too severe-maybe a 2 or a 3 at most.
No signs of an obstructed airway. We'll keep it clear for now.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Respiratory rate is 18 now-shallow, even, and unlabored.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Room air oxygen saturation is at 76%. It may need improvement through oxygen administration.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Administered 2-3 liters of O2 through nasal cannula to help raise oxygen. Saturation is up to 93%.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pulse rate and quality
dialogue.utterance: Pulse is 89-strong, regular, and palpable on both sides.
Blood pressure is abnormally high-currently 242 over 140.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: No signs of an abnormal breath pattern. She feels better when on oxygen now. 35. Secondary Assessment; skin exam - Color: Pallor, diaphoresis; EMT: You mentioned abdominal pain didn't occur. Have you noticed any paleness or sweating lately?
No, I feel fairly okay. Just been off for the last few days.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Assessment of mental status
dialogue.utterance: Let me check your recall again. What day is it again?

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Assessment of mental status
dialogue.utterance: Thursday again.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Neuro - Hemiplegia Post CVA
dialogue.utterance: You can still move on the right side-left side weak from your stroke, yes?

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Neuro - Hemiplegia Post CVA
dialogue.utterance: Your hemiplegia status is something we must be mindful of during moving and securing you for transport.
Have you experienced any other abdominal discomfort?
No, I haven't. Just the shortness of breath and some mild discomfort in my chest area.
Pulse remains consistent at 89-strong and regular but on the higher side.
She's breathing at 16-still steady and unlabored with supplemental oxygen.
Her blood pressure is now 239 over 125-still extremely high and concerning for us to monitor closely.
Oxygen saturation is 93%, maintained well with the 2-3 liters given.
Tried to measure blood glucose, but the meter returned 0-haven't seen any abnormal glucose readings yet.
EKG currently shows a sinus rhythm with high voltages and mild signs of left ventricular hypertrophy detected.
I need to apply a 4-lead EKG now. You okay with that, Grace?
4-lead EKG applied and shows sinus rhythm normally on the monitor.
Now time for a 12-lead EKG. Will give the care team more precise findings about your heart function.
12-lead EKG completed-some anterior lead deviations observed along with minimal signs of infarction noted.
Based on protocol, we are managing this as a medical condition-nausea/vomiting under protocol 3-9.
Checking saturation again-remains at 93%.
No signs of any declining oxygen levels or dyspnea worsening.
You had mentioned you always go to [COMPANY_NAME]-still okay with going there tonight?
Yes, I always go there. I'm used to the team and procedures they use.
Two-person pivot from the chair to stretcher staged now.
Secured her and ensured rails are in lock-stretcher prepped and ready to move.
Taking BP once again-still abnormally high at 239 over 125.
Pulse recording is at 84, still elevated but steady.
Checking EKG-pattern consistent with first reading.
No new deviations-EKG findings remain the same.
All restraints validated. Ready for safe transfer to transport.
Confirmed all straps, rails, and oxygen lines secured for safe transport.
She remains on 2-3L oxygen via nasal cannula and on protocol 3-9.
Reassessing her breathing rate-still 16, and unlabored.
Still no signs of respiratory distress or new conditions present.
No new interventions or medications applied, with all findings stable.
All findings unchanged-fully ready for transport.
All lines and restraints secured-just starting transport.
All data and stabilization provided to ED in room 14.
Patient remains on protocol 3-9 for medical-nausea/vomiting.
All EKG documentation and care notes handed over to the care team.
Transport and transfer are complete-here at the hospital.
